book_claims.claim: Sardines recommended as a high omega-3, omega-6, and vitamin B12 fish; low in mercury
book_claims.mechanism: Small oily fish with dense omega-3 content and low mercury bioaccumulation due to short lifespan and small size

book_claims.claim: Sardines are rich in vitamin B12 (6.8 mcg per 75 g canned, 113% DV), calcium (325 mg per 3 oz canned with bones, 33% DV), omega-3 (0.74–1.05 g EPA/DHA per 75 g canned), iron (2 mg per 3 oz, 11% DV), and vitamin D (46 IU per 2 sardines, 11.5% DV)
book_claims.mechanism: Whole fish with bones provides calcium, vitamin D, omega-3 EPA/DHA, and vitamin B12 simultaneously

supplementary_research.finding: Regular sardine consumption (PMID 21034225) significantly improved HDL/LDL ratios and reduced triglycerides in overweight adults over 5 months
Small pelagic fish like sardines have among the lowest methylmercury concentrations of any seafood (PMID 33096723), confirming the book's low-mercury recommendation
Sardines deliver exceptional nutrient density per calorie: whole-bone calcium, heme iron, coenzyme Q10, and complete protein alongside omega-3s, making them a longevity superfood

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highly bioavailable marine omega-3s; packing oil further aids absorption
Bone-derived calcium with high bioavailability (~30%); co-present vitamin D enhances uptake
Mitochondrial antioxidant; fat-soluble, enhanced by co-ingested oil
Fat-soluble; absorbed well with the fish's own oil content

synergies.description: Both small oily fish recommended together for high omega-3 and low mercury; often used together as pizza topping in The Longevity Diet
Vitamin C in lemon enhances non-heme iron absorption from sardines
Olive oil complements omega-3s with oleocanthal anti-inflammatory polyphenols; aids fat-soluble vitamin absorption
